# Decision-Grade AI

> A framework for executives, technology leaders, and strategy functions working with AI in 2026. Built around verification: what to demand from AI vendors, what to build inside your organization, and what to watch over the next eighteen months.

This site is the canonical reference for the Decision-Grade AI framework. The argument starts from a single observation: AI is already in your analytical pipeline somewhere, whether formally authorized or not. The token-level production cost collapsed by two to three orders of magnitude. The all-in cost of analytical work, once human prompting, editing, and integration are counted, fell by roughly 5x to 20x. Either lens tells the same story: more analytical artifacts move through any given reviewer than was possible two years ago. The cost of verifying that the reasoning underneath holds has not moved at the same rate, particularly at the structural-reasoning layer. The gap is the operational risk most executive AI guidance does not yet address.

The framework is published openly because the Zero Trust posture it advocates extends to the doctrine itself. You should not have to trust the publisher. You can verify the framework, contest it, fork it, or implement it elsewhere.

## Framework pages

- [Introduction](https://decision-grade.ai/introduction): Who the framework is for (CEOs/COOs/boards, CIOs/CTOs/CISOs, Chief Strategy/Foresight), how to read it, the three verifiability surfaces (llms.txt, llms-full.txt, MCP server), and where to start.
- [The Frame](https://decision-grade.ai/the-frame): The diagnosis. Why most executive AI guidance is scoped to the wrong problem (hallucinations). What the real problem is (verification deficit at the structural-reasoning layer). The counterthesis worth engaging (some verification cost is collapsing too; the bottleneck migrates upward in the stack). Why existing controls do not catch the failure. Five strawman examples of well-cited consulting prose with different inferential failures (survivorship bias, mechanism substitution, aggregation across incomparable populations, reverse causation, trend extrapolation past the data). The historical parallel to pre-2008 credit ratings, with three candidate transmission paths (regulatory enforcement, litigation exposure, public failure events).
- [The Evidence Base](https://decision-grade.ai/evidence): The empirical case. Measured AI error rates as of May 2026 across four error modes (fabrication, misgrounding, omission, reasoning failure) and three measurement tiers (atomic factuality, domain-task hallucination, multi-step reasoning failure). Seven anchor sources with named primary citations: Mass General Brigham JAMA Network Open clinical-reasoning study (90-100% differential-diagnosis failure across 21 LLMs); Cheng et al. Science sycophancy paper (49% over-affirmation across 11 frontier models); Damien Charlotin's AI Hallucination Cases Database (1,453 court decisions, May 2026); Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index (benchmark saturation outpacing evaluation); Vectara HHEM-2.3 (grounded-summarization hallucination 3-13% for flagship models); UK AISI Frontier AI Trends Report (every model jailbroken, 62,000+ harmful behaviors); Opper car wash test (42 of 53 frontier models failed an implicit-constraint reasoning prompt). The car-wash diagnostic and its business analogues. Honest caveats. What is not yet measured (the meta-point).
- [The Doctrine](https://decision-grade.ai/the-doctrine): The posture. Zero Trust applied to AI verification, framed as analogy to NIST 800-207 rather than derivation. Three layers: Independence (no AI family verifies its own work; vendor independence is necessary but not sufficient), Doctrine (rules enforced architecturally; overrides require break-glass authorization, not silence), Accountability (every decision survives independent challenge through tamper-evident records). What cryptographic anchoring proves and what it does not (integrity, not validity).
- [The Buyer's Checklist](https://decision-grade.ai/buyers-checklist): The action. Seven procurement questions to put to any AI verification vendor. What a serious answer looks like, what a worrying answer looks like, red flags, and a provisional five-point scoring grid. The single-sentence test: "Can I verify your verdicts without having to trust you?" A note that no vendor currently scores full marks; the buyer's lever works by asking, not by waiting.
- [Lane Discipline](https://decision-grade.ai/lane-discipline): The internal practice. How to separate decision-grade outputs from volume-grade outputs inside an organization. Classification at point of production, routing rules, four failure modes (unlabeled slip, theater inversion, verification lock-in, attention inversion), five operational specifics to decide upfront, and the single board-level metric to track.
- [2026 Watchlist](https://decision-grade.ai/watchlist): The calendar. Five scenario trajectories (Polished Flood / Audit Islands / Procurement of Proof / Compliance Theater / Validator's Edge), two levers active this quarter, one per-organization deadline (90 days from the day your team adopted AI-assisted drafting), one thesis falsifier (AI volume rises, unanchored-claim rate does not, with a frank acknowledgment that the falsifier is currently hard to operationalize). Regulatory signals: SR 26-2 (with the caveat that generative and agentic AI are out of scope), GENIUS Act, NIST AI RMF in federal procurement, EU AI Act (high-risk standalone systems from Dec 2, 2027; integrated into products from Aug 2, 2028).
- [About](https://decision-grade.ai/about): Author, stake disclosure (VALIS Systems), framework commitments (verifiable, forkable, contestable, testable).
- [MCP Server](https://decision-grade.ai/mcp): Connection instructions for Claude Desktop, Cursor, or any MCP-aware AI client. Endpoint: https://decision-grade.ai/api/mcp.

## Publisher

Published by VALIS Systems. The publisher has a commercial interest in the doctrine being adopted. The doctrine itself is independent of any specific product. Reference: https://valissystems.com.

Content is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). Share, adapt, and build upon the work with attribution.

## Repository

Source: https://github.com/DavidVALIS/decision-grade

Substantive disagreements and corrections are welcome via issues and pull requests. The doctrine improves when it is contested.
